subject: Request for Comment: March 2023 FOMC Rate Decision and Forward Guidance
body: I am a financial correspondent at the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve's ongoing rate-hiking cycle. Following the FOMC's decision to raise the federal funds rate by 25 basis points at the March 2023 meeting, I would like to request an on-record comment from FRBSF President Mary Daly regarding the pace of future tightening. Specifically, I am interested in whether the banking sector stress observed in mid-March influenced the Committee's deliberations. Please let me know if a spokesperson is available for a brief interview before our 5 PM EST publication deadline.

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising interest rates when people can't afford their mortgages?
body: I just want to understand why the Federal Reserve keeps making it harder for regular people to buy a home. My mortgage rate went from 3% to almost 7% in less than two years and I had to cancel my home purchase. I read that the Fed is doing this to fight inflation, but groceries are still expensive too so it doesn't seem to be working. Can someone please explain in plain English what the plan is and when rates might come down?

subject: Formal Inquiry Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Community Banks
body: On behalf of the California Community Bankers Association, we are writing to formally request clarification on how the proposed Basel III Endgame rules, as outlined in the July 2023 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, will be applied to institutions with total consolidated assets below $100 billion. Our member institutions are particularly concerned about the operational risk capital surcharge and its potential to constrain small business lending in underserved markets throughout California and the broader Twelfth Federal Reserve District. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss the anticipated compliance timeline and any available phase-in provisions. A written response within 30 business days would be greatly appreciated.

subject: Press Inquiry: FRBSF Economic Letter on Labor Market Tightness and Wage Inflation
body: This is a follow-up to a voicemail left earlier today for the FRBSF communications office. I am a reporter with Bloomberg News seeking to speak with one of the authors of the recently published FRBSF Economic Letter examining the relationship between labor market tightness and wage-driven inflation pressures. We are preparing a feature piece ahead of the next jobs report and would like to discuss the paper's findings on nominal wage growth and its implications for the Fed's dual mandate. A callback at your earliest convenience would be much appreciated.

subject: Question about the Fed's 2% inflation target - who decided that number?
body: Hi, I'm a retired teacher and I've been trying to learn more about how the Federal Reserve works since inflation hit my fixed income pretty hard over the past few years. I keep hearing about the 2% inflation target but nobody ever explains where that number came from or why it isn't 1% or 3%. Did Congress set that number, or did the Fed just pick it on their own? I would really appreciate a simple explanation.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: Constituent Concerns Regarding FRBSF Supervision of Silicon Valley Bank
body: This correspondence is submitted on behalf of Representative Jill Chen of the 17th Congressional District of California. In light of the March 10, 2023 failure of Silicon Valley Bank, our office has received numerous constituent inquiries questioning the adequacy of supervisory oversight by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. We respectfully request a detailed written response addressing the timeline of FRBSF supervisory actions taken between January 2022 and March 2023, including any Matters Requiring Attention issued to SVB's management. Additionally, we ask whether the FRBSF has implemented any changes to its supervisory framework for large regional banks as a result of the post-mortem review conducted by Vice Chair Barr.
sender_organization: Office of Representative Jill Chen, CA-17

subject: Is the Fed going to crash the economy on purpose to bring inflation down?
body: I keep reading that the Fed might cause a recession to get inflation under control and I'm worried about my job in the tech sector which is already seeing layoffs. It feels like regular workers are being asked to pay the price for inflation that was caused by supply chain problems and corporate greed. Is there any way to fight inflation without making unemployment go up? I thought the Fed was supposed to care about both inflation AND jobs.

subject: Request for Engagement: Impact of QT on Agency MBS Market Liquidity in the Twelfth District
body: Pacific Coast Mortgage Investors LLC is writing to request a technical engagement session with FRBSF economists regarding the Federal Reserve's ongoing quantitative tightening program and its observed effects on agency mortgage-backed securities market liquidity and spreads. Since the Fed began allowing up to $35 billion in MBS to roll off its balance sheet monthly beginning in September 2022, we have observed meaningful widening in primary-secondary spreads that is disproportionately affecting borrowers in higher-cost housing markets across California, Oregon, and Washington. We would welcome the opportunity to share our market data with FRBSF research staff and to receive any available guidance on how QT trajectory decisions will incorporate housing market dynamics. Please advise on the appropriate point of contact within your institution.

subject: Data Request: FRBSF FedViews Publication and Q4 2024 Economic Projections
body: I am an economics reporter with the San Francisco Chronicle preparing a year-end analysis of the Twelfth Federal Reserve District's economic performance and outlook. I am requesting access to the most recent FRBSF FedViews publication as well as any updated staff projections for GDP growth, unemployment, and PCE inflation for the Q4 2024 and full-year 2025 forecast horizon. I am also interested in whether FRBSF economists see particular downside risks to the economic outlook from slowing global trade given the district's significant exposure to Pacific Rim commerce. Please let me know if an economist is available for a background briefing.

subject: Complaint Regarding Overdraft Fee Practices at a Large National Bank
body: I am writing to file a formal complaint against my bank, which continues to charge $35 overdraft fees even after the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau issued guidance discouraging such practices. I was charged four overdraft fees in a single day totaling $140 on transactions of less than $20 each, which I believe is exploitative and harmful to low-income customers. I am not sure if the Federal Reserve or the CFPB is the right agency to contact, but I was told the Fed supervises large bank holding companies. I would appreciate guidance on whether your office can investigate this matter or direct me to the appropriate regulatory body.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Neutral Rate Estimates
body: I'm a financial correspondent at the Wall Street Journal working on a feature about diverging views among Fed officials on the neutral rate of interest. Following President Daly's recent speech at the Brookings Institution, I'd like to request a formal comment from the FRBSF communications office regarding her current estimate of r-star and how it informs the Bank's policy stance. Could you also confirm whether President Daly plans to vote on the FOMC in the upcoming rotation cycle? My deadline for this piece is end of business Friday.

subject: Why are mortgage rates so high if the Fed is done raising rates?
body: I don't understand how the Federal Reserve can say it's done with rate hikes but my mortgage lender is still quoting me 7.5% on a 30-year fixed loan. My family is trying to buy a home in Sacramento and it feels impossible right now. Can someone explain the connection between what the Fed does and what the banks actually charge regular people? I feel like the system is rigged against working families.

subject: Community Development Financial Institution Concerns Regarding CRA Modernization Rule
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Credit Union, I am writing to formally express our institution's concerns regarding the final Community Reinvestment Act modernization rule issued jointly by the OCC, FDIC, and Federal Reserve in October 2023. Specifically, we are seeking clarification on the assessment area requirements as they apply to credit unions operating primarily through digital channels in underserved rural communities across California and Nevada. We believe the current framework may inadvertently disadvantage smaller CDFIs that lack the branch infrastructure of larger commercial banks, and we respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss implementation guidance. We have attached a summary of our concerns and a draft comment letter for your review.

subject: Follow-up on January CPI Data and Fed Rate Cut Timeline
body: This is a voicemail from Maria Chen at Bloomberg News following up on our earlier request for an interview. The January CPI print came in hotter than expected this morning at 3.1% year-over-year, and markets have sharply repriced the expected timing of the first Fed rate cut. We'd like to speak with an FRBSF economist or spokesperson today about whether this data changes the calculus for the March FOMC meeting. We're also interested in the Bank's view on shelter inflation persistence and its role in keeping headline CPI elevated. Please call back at your earliest convenience.

subject: Concerned about job market after Fed rate hikes
body: I work in the tech sector in the Bay Area and have been laid off twice in the past 18 months. I've read that the Fed deliberately tries to cool the labor market to fight inflation, and I'm writing to ask if the people making these decisions understand what this means for real workers. My unemployment benefits have run out and the job market in my field has not recovered the way I expected. I just want to know if the Federal Reserve considers the human impact of its employment mandate decisions.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: QT Balance Sheet Runoff Impact on Treasury Market Liquidity
body: Dear FRBSF Leadership, this correspondence is submitted on behalf of Congressman Kevin Larson, member of the House Financial Services Committee, regarding the Federal Reserve's ongoing quantitative tightening program and its potential effects on Treasury market functioning. The Committee has received testimony from primary dealers expressing concern that the combined pace of Fed balance sheet runoff and elevated Treasury issuance may be straining repo market liquidity. We respectfully request a technical briefing from Federal Reserve staff prior to the Committee's scheduled hearing on February 28th. Please direct your response to my office at the address listed below.
sender_organization: Office of Congressman Kevin Larson, U.S. House of Representatives

subject: Question about FDIC insurance and bank failures
body: After what happened with Silicon Valley Bank last year, I'm really worried about whether my money is safe. I have about $280,000 spread across two accounts at a mid-size regional bank here in California. Can the Federal Reserve guarantee my deposits are protected, and what exactly is the difference between the Fed and the FDIC when it comes to protecting regular depositors? I saw on the news that the government stepped in to cover deposits above $250,000 for SVB customers but I don't know if that would happen for everyone.

subject: Data Request: Federal Funds Rate Historical Series for Economic Research
body: I am reaching out on behalf of the research division at Western Asset Management Company to request access to high-frequency federal funds rate data and FRBSF working paper archives related to interest rate pass-through mechanisms. We are conducting internal research on the transmission lag between policy rate adjustments and commercial lending rates, with a particular focus on the 2022-2024 tightening cycle. Could you direct us to the appropriate data portal or research contact? We are also interested in whether any upcoming FRBSF Economic Letters will address this topic in the near term.

subject: FRBSF Economic Forecast Request for Q3 2025 Outlook Story
body: Hello, I'm a reporter with the San Francisco Chronicle preparing a regional economic outlook feature for our summer edition covering California's economic trajectory through the end of 2025. I'd like to request the most recent FRBSF projections for regional GDP growth, unemployment, and inflation, and ask if a senior economist would be available for a brief on-the-record interview about risks to the outlook including potential tariff impacts and housing market dynamics. I'm particularly interested in how the FRBSF views divergence between California's labor market and national trends. My publication deadline is June 15th.

subject: Complaint Regarding Unfair Overdraft Fee Practices at National Bank
body: I am writing to file a formal complaint against First National Bank of California for what I believe are deceptive and predatory overdraft fee practices. Over the course of three months, I was charged $140 in overdraft fees on transactions that my bank cleared in a manipulated order to maximize the number of fees assessed, despite my account having sufficient funds at the time of the original purchase. I have spoken with the bank's customer service department twice without resolution and was told to contact a regulator. I am requesting that the Federal Reserve investigate this practice and advise me on what consumer protection remedies may be available to me.
